coredata.dc:description: A comprehensive prediction for molecular power conversion efficiency and designing novel sensitizers are effective strategies for preparing highly efficient dye-sensitized solar cell devices. In this work, four high-efficiency D-π-A type triazatruxene (TAT) based organic dyes have been designed by changing spacers and anchoring groups of the reference molecule. A global evaluating model that illustrates the micro-mechanism of enhanced molecular photoelectric performances through importing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was built. Upon incorporating GQDs, an obvious absorption peak with high molar extinction coefficients appeared around the ultraviolet region. Furthermore, molecular light-harvesting efficiency (LHE) in the visible region has increased, and molecular charge transfer performance enhanced, and molecular regeneration ability improved, which contributed to the improved photocurrent (Jsc) and power conversion efficiency (PCE). Molecular ZL003 through the global evaluating model showed (Jsc = 19.03 mA cm−2, Voc = 0.874 V, PCE = 14.5%) are in accordance with the experimental data (Jsc = 19.74 mA cm−2, Voc = 0.957 V, PCE = 13.4%). The model can provide a basis for evaluating molecular photoelectric performance. The micro-mechanism that the GQDs enhance molecular photoelectric performances has been revealed in the framework of GQDs photo current-voltage evaluating model.

head.ce:title: How graphene strengthened molecular photoelectric performance of solar cells: A photo current-voltage assessment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A comprehensive prediction for molecular power conversion efficiency and designing novel sensitizers are effective strategies for preparing highly efficient dye-sensitized solar cell devices. In this work, four high-efficiency D-π-A type triazatruxene (TAT) based organic dyes have been designed by changing spacers and anchoring groups of the reference molecule. A global evaluating model that illustrates the micro-mechanism of enhanced molecular photoelectric performances through importing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was built. Upon incorporating GQDs, an obvious absorption peak with high molar extinction coefficients appeared around the ultraviolet region. Furthermore, molecular light-harvesting efficiency (LHE) in the visible region has increased, and molecular charge transfer performance enhanced, and molecular regeneration ability improved, which contributed to the improved photocurrent (Jsc) and power conversion efficiency (PCE). Molecular ZL003 through the global evaluating model showed (Jsc = 19.03 mA cm−2, Voc = 0.874 V, PCE = 14.5%) are in accordance with the experimental data (Jsc = 19.74 mA cm−2, Voc = 0.957 V, PCE = 13.4%). The model can provide a basis for evaluating molecular photoelectric performance. The micro-mechanism that the GQDs enhance molecular photoelectric performances has been revealed in the framework of GQDs photo current-voltage evaluating model.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution and energy crisis are the most acute concerns around the world (Oziolor et al., 2019). The ideal way to deal with these threats is to develop renewable energy production methods (Shindell and Smith, 2019; Ju et al., 2020). Solar cells are under development due to their vast potential application. Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) have drawn much attention as the third generation of solar cells due to their secure fabricating mechanism and ideal PCE (Cole et al., 2019; Ciou et al., 2017). The composition of DSSCs includes electrodes, TiO2, dyes, and a redox couple. The working processes include electron transition, injection, collection and molecular regeneration (Liu et al., 2018). O'Regan and Grätzel (1991) reported PCE of DSSCs achieved 7.1%, which brings a new channel for the development of DSSCs. With the development of DSSCs, quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs) draw much interest for their high efficiency and low cost (Zhang et al., 2011). GQDs can induce significant extinction coefficients upon light absorption and has been widely applied in QDSSCs to improve molecular photovoltaic performance (Huang et al., 2019; Patil et al., 2019). The lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) is primarily affected by the acceptors of dyes (Samiee and Hossienpour, 2019) and directly determines the performances of electrons injection between photosensitizer and the surface of TiO2. The strategy of tuning molecular LUMO by modifying acceptor groups is available to build highly efficient dyes (Guo et al., 2010).

Recently, the performances of N719 based DSSCs were studied, according to Salam et al report (Salam et al., 2015). When introducing GQDs, GQD/N719 composites exhibited a higher PCE of 6.22% than pure N719. The reason is due to the improved molecular charge-transfer nature, the increased dye adsorption, the reduced charge recombination and the increased light-capturing property. Molecular photoelectric properties of C106 dye with GQDs (Kim et al., 2016) were reported by Teymourinia et al. (2018). Results found that GQD/Dye composites exhibited strengthened light-capturing properties and led to a 21% enhancement in JSC than the reference cell (Kim et al., 2016; Teymourinia et al., 2018). Several indeno[1,2-b]indole-based D-π-A type organic molecules have been synthesized by Qian et al. (2017) by introducing different spacers and anchoring groups. Results found that molecular LUMO has been tuned, and QX18 with 2-(1,1-dicyano methylene)rhodanine (DCRD) exhibited lower LUMO and higher electrons injection performance that led to the improved JSC of 11.9 mA cm−2. The LUMO levels of four novel metal-free dyes (AFL7-AFL10) with or without benzothiadiazole auxiliary acceptor showed noticeable differences according to the reports of Xie et al. (2019). AFL7 based on the bithiophene bridge exhibited the best JSC of 16.9 mA cm−2 and PCE of 7.92% under standard AM 1.5G sunlight.

Theoretical investigation can provide a basis for experimental synthesis. Recently, Gao et al. (2019) reported the photoelectric properties of hybrid nanocomposites of GQDs and carbazole and carbazole dyes based on time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) and DFT. Results showed that composites' photoelectric performances were improved due to the introduced GQDs that enhanced molecular LHE in the visible region. Sharma and Jha (2019) studied the effects of adatoms on carboxy edge-functionalized graphene dot on molecular photoelectric performances of QDSSCs within DFT theory. Results found that after modifying the GQDs by doping phosphorous, maximum efficiency was increased due to the improved electron-donating nature that facilitates electrons injection performance. Kandregula et al. (2020) researched a series of boron dipyromethene ancillary acceptor based dyes in DSSCs. Results demonstrated that molecular with boron dipyrromethene ancillary acceptor presented a decreased LUMO for effective electrons injection, and D2 with carbazole donor group exhibited the highest efficiency. Two D-π-A type carbazole organic dyes (MK1 and MK2) contain different anchoring groups were studied by Elroby and Jedidi (2020) through DFT and TDDFT method. Results demonstrate that molecular LUMO was affected by the changing of anchoring groups. Molecular MK2 exhibited robust intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) properties due to the replacement with the hydroxamic acid anchoring group.

Stimulated by the recent report of highly efficient D-π-A type triazatruxene dye of ZL003 (with high PCE of 13.4%) (Zhang et al., 2020), we estimated photo current-voltage (I-V) model, and the simulated PCE of ZL003 (14.5%) is closed to experimental PCE of (13.4%). Furthermore, the model contains the molecular designed strategy by introducing furan and cyanoacetic acids, and four TAT based molecules (see Fig. 1 ) were developed. Notably, the influences of graphene quantum dots on the photoelectric properties are analyzed in-depth, and the enhanced micro-mechanism by controlling the GQDs has been revealed. Such base knowledge can help us understand how the GQDs improves molecular photoelectric performance.

The Geometrical structures simulation is based on DFT/B3LYP/6-31G(d, p) method (Malmir and Vosoughi, 2013) with the (CPCM) model (Huang et al., 2017) in an ethanol solvent. Dye/(TiO2)9 complexes are simulated through genecp method within LASDA basis set for Ti atoms and 6-31 g (d, p) for C, H, O, N, and S atoms (He et al., 2016; Gou et al., 2014). TDDFT/CAM-B3LYP/6-31 g (d, p) methods were applied to obtain molecular excitation properties (Yanai et al., 2004). Multiwfn 3.6 program was implemented to study molecular (ICT) performances, the heat map of hole and electrons composition, electrostatic potential (ESP), frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs), and local orbital locator (LOL) performances (Ashraf et al., 2017). The interfragmentary charge transfer (IFCT) method was applied to observe the transferred electrons upon molecular excitation (Ashraf et al., 2017). NBO 3.0.1 program in the gaussian 09 was employed to analyze molecular natural bond orbital (NBO) performances (Glendening et al., xxxx). Gaussian sum program was used for the partial density of states (PDOS) analysis to learn the movement of the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 and to obtain molecular photovoltage (Nagarajan et al., 2017). Marcus theory was applied to determine molecular electron injection performances. Newns-Anderson model was utilized to get electron injection time (Preat, 2010). Fukui function distribution on the local molecular surface was acquired with VDW and multiwfn 3.6 (Ashraf et al., 2017). Molecular recombination distances were investigated to illustrate molecular recombination performance (Li et al., 2016). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained to reflect molecular vibration (Legler et al., 2015). All calculations are based on the gaussian 09 package (Gaussian et al., 2009).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduced molecular HOMO energy levels by the introduction of graphene
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S1 and Fig. S2 present the optimized molecular geometry structures. The triple bonds connected π-bridge with acceptors play an essential role in molecular charge transfer performance. The obtained LOL map (Ashraf et al., 2017) in Fig. S3 and Fig. S4 focusing on the molecular triple bonds shows that the triple bond occupies high value (in red color), demonstrating that the triple bond can cause better molecular charge transfer performances. The functions of tripe bonds are to act as bridges and connect firmly with donor parts and acceptor parts. So, the excited electrons can be fluently transferred from the donor parts to the acceptor parts upon molecular excitation. The induced better molecular intramolecular charge transfer performance by the triple bonds is beneficial for improving molecular photoelectric performance. Table 1 and Fig. 2 show that all molecular LUMO are higher than the CB of TiO2 (−4.0 eV), and all highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) is lower than redox couple potential (−4.8 eV). Those results illustrate that all studied molecules can cause a photoelectric response in DSSCs (Hagberg et al., 2007).

Fig. S5 shows the LUMO of single dyes and GQD/Dye composites are nearly the same. In contrast, the HOMO of all GQD/Dye composites decreased, which leads to more significant gaps between molecular HOMO and redox couple. The phenomenon is beneficial for the regeneration ability of GQD/Dye composites and contributing to the improvement of molecular JSC. Table 1 and Fig. 2 show the LUMO of single dyes and GQD/Dye composites are in the same reduced trend: ZL003 (−2.830 eV) > ZL006 (−2.843 eV) > ZL007 (−2.978 eV) > ZL008 (−2.996 eV) > ZL009 (−3.137 eV); GQD/ZL003 (−2.838 eV) > GQD/ZL006 (−2.849 eV) > GQD/ZL007 (−2.979 eV) > GQD/ZL008 (−3.001 eV) > GQD/ZL009 (−3.143 eV). Therefore, it can be concluded that, compared with ZL003, replacing the benzene ring with furan can cause reduced LUMO level, replacing the methane acid with cyanoacetic acid can cause reduced LUMO level compared to the strategy of replacing the benzene ring. Introducing furan and cyanoacetic acid simultaneously can cause a reduced LUMO level than just replacing the spacer or anchoring group. It is worth noting that the strategy of just changing benzoic acid with a single cyanoacetic acid shows the lowest LUMO energy levels. Therefore, by the strategy of modifying the molecular acceptor group, molecular LUMO can be tuned and molecular electron injection performance can be improved. ZL009 with the lowest LUMO should exhibit the best electron injection performance.

The PDOS can vividly show HOMO and LUMO's contribution by different parts of molecules (Wang et al., 2020). FMOs shows the distribution of holes and electrons of molecular HOMO and LUMO (Grätzel, 2001). NBO can quantitatively present the transferred charge between different parts of the investigated molecules. All molecular HOMO and LUMO are mainly contributed by bridge and acceptor from Fig. S6 (a) (b) (d) (e), Table S1, Table S2 and FMOs in Fig. S7 and Fig. S8. When introducing GQDs, GQDs have nearly no contribution to molecular HOMO and LUMO orbitals. To better illustrate each part of molecular acceptors' function, electrons attracting the ability of the triple bond, spacer and anchoring group were qualitatively analyzed from NBO and PDOS. Upon excitation, acceptors of ZL007, ZL008 and Zl009 have attracted electrons of 0.00226, 0.01224, 0.05856, respectively. At the same time, that of ZL003 and ZL006 have lost electrons of 0.03069 and 0.03388 (in Table S3), which is because that cyanoacetic acid has a better molecular electron attracting ability than methanic acid. Each part's function can also be reflected by the contribution for LUMO from Table S4 and Fig. S6 (c) and (f). Cyanoacetic acid exhibits much better electron attracting ability than methanic acid reflected by the large contribution for LUMO. The contribution for molecular LUMO of anchoring groups presents the following trend: [ZL009/(GQD/ZL009)] (24%) > [ZL007/(GQD/ZL007)] (22%) > [ZL008/(GQD/ZL008)] (20%) > [ZL006/(GQD/ZL006)] (1%) = [ZL003/(GQD/ZL003)] (1%); such phenomenon can be attributed to fact that the cyanoacetic acid has better electron attracting ability than methanic acid. The anchoring group of cyanoacetic acid containing additional negative functional group (CN) has facilitated better electron attracting ability of cyanoacetic acid than methanic acid. The cyanoacetic acid functional group can convey more electrons to the TiO2 conduction band compared to methanic acid through the bidentate cheating channel, which helps to improve molecular photoelectric performance. Therefore, it can be drawn that the anchoring group of cyanoacetic acid can facilitate molecular charge transfer performance due to its better electron attracting nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The appearance of different high absorption peaks by introducing graphene
ce:section.ce:para: Significant absorption peaks mean better molecular sunlight utilizing ability. Table S5, Table S6, and Fig. 3 exhibit the excitation performances of studied single dyes and GQD/Dye composites. Table S7 shows that the obtained absorption peak of ZL003 (514.17 nm) is in accordance with experimental data (519 nm) (Zhang et al., 2020). The absorption peaks of single dyes present the following order: ZL009 (538.30 nm) > ZL008 (535.12 nm) > ZL007 (524.93 nm) > ZL006 (516.52 nm) > ZL003 (514.17 nm), and that of GQD/Dye composites have the same trend as single dyes. So it can be concluded that, compared with ZL003, replacing the benzene ring with furan can cause a slight red-shift of the molecular absorption spectrum; changing the methanic acid with cyanoacetic acid can induce red-shifted molecular absorption spectrums and improved molar extinction coefficient than replacing the benzene ring with furan; simultaneously, introducing furan and cyanoacetic acid can cause enhanced red-shifted spectrum and increased molar extinction coefficient than just changing the spacer or anchoring group; replacing the benzoic acid with cyanoacetic acid showed the biggest red-shift spectrum among the designed dyes. ZL007 (GQD/ZL007), ZL008 (GQD/ZL008), and ZL009 (GQD/ZL009) within cyanoacetic acid showed red-shifted spectrums than ZL006 (GQD/ZL006) and ZL003 (GQD/ZL003), which is owing to the better electron attracting ability of cyanoacetic acid that induced better molecular charge transfer performance and facilitated the red-shift of absorption spectrums. Upon the introduction of GQDs, although nearly none of the absorption peaks of studied molecules have changed, there still appears a distinct absorption peak with a high molar extinction coefficient near the ultraviolet region, which strengthened molecular sunlight using ability and enhanced molecular LHE. Therefore, all GQD/Dye composites should have improved JSC and PCE than single dyes. Especially for GQD/ZL009, which should generate the largest JSC due to the significant absorption peaks and relatively higher LHE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced intramolecular charge transfer performance by introducing graphene
ce:section.ce:para: Molecular excitation type plays an important role in molecular ICT performance. All investigated single Dye/TiO2 and GQD/Dye/TiO2 complexes show small D and large Sr values. Especially for all molecular t index with a negative value from Table S8. Thus, all investigated systems belong to local excitation (Fahim et al., 2018). The local excitation mainly occurred on donor and π-bridge. The contribution of π-bridge for holes are as following: ZL003 (83.57%) > ZL006 (82.16%) > ZL007 (80.23%) > ZL009 (79.57%) > ZL008 (74.62%); while π-bridge contribution for electrons are in the following: ZL003 (92.13%) > ZL006 (91.69%) > ZL007 (85.12%) > ZL009 (79.75%) > ZL008 (77.57%) from Table S9. Table S10 shows that the GQD/Dye composites have the same trend as single dyes. Therefore, upon excitation, electrons mainly transferred from the donor to π-bridge, and the charge mainly focused on π-bridge. The overlap map (in Fig. S9) and the charge difference density (CDD) (in Fig. S10) show that the transferred charge mainly focused on π-bridge (Zhao et al., 2020). The charge transferring distances of GQD/Dye/TiO2 complexes increased than Dye/TiO2 complexes from Fig. 4 : GQD/ZL003/TiO2 (3.120 Å) > ZL003/TiO2 (3.003 Å); GQD/ZL006/TiO2 (2.078 Å) > ZL006/TiO2 (2.024 Å); GQD/ZL007/TiO2 (2.725 Å) > ZL007/TiO2 (2.586 Å); GQD/ZL008/TiO2 (2.625 Å) > ZL008/TiO2 (2.376 Å); GQD/ZL009/TiO2 (3.471 Å) > ZL009/TiO2 (3.455 Å). So, it can be concluded that the introduced GQDs enhanced electrons' charge transfer performance and should produce improved molecular photoelectric performances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Increased molecular chemical resistance by the introduction of graphene
ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectric performances of investigated dyes can be evaluated through molecular chemical reactivity parameters. The high value of electrons affinity (EA) is beneficial for electron transportation. Little chemical hardness (h) contributes to the improved photocurrent. Higher electrophilicity (W) means that molecules have better molecular electron-donating capacity. Higher electron-accepting power (W+) means that molecules have better stability when attracting electrons from the outer environment (Parr and Szentpály, 1999). Therefore, higher EA, W, (W+) and lower h should induce better charge transfer performance and generate higher JSC. Table 2 and Fig. 5 show the chemical reactivity data and order of investigated molecules. The EA is in the following order: ZL009 (3.2704 eV) > ZL008 (3.1146 eV) > ZL007 (3.0828 eV) > ZL006 (2.9429 eV) > ZL003 (2.9299 eV); W is as following: ZL009 (10.2436 eV) > ZL008 (9.0938 eV) > ZL007 (8.8559 eV) > ZL006 (7.9578 eV) > ZL003 (7.8798 eV); (W + ) is followed by ZL009 (8.3030 eV) > ZL008 (7.2083 eV) > ZL007 (6.9800 eV) > ZL006 (6.1285 eV) > ZL003 (6.0547 eV); h presents the order of ZL003 (0.9603 eV) > ZL006 (0.9543 eV) > ZL007 (0.8920 eV) > ZL008 (0.8752 eV) > ZL009 (0.8145 eV). Upon the introduction of GQDs, all of the values except for h of the GQD/Dye composites have slightly increased owing to the increased molecular structure; the order of simulated chemical reactivity indexes of GQD/Dye composites are kept in consistent with the single dyes from Fig. S11. Thus, it can be drawn that, compared with ZL003, replacing the benzene ring with furan produced improved molecular charge transfer and photoelectric performance; replacing methane acid group with cyanoacetic acid shows an enhanced molecular charge transfer and JSC performance rather than the strategy to modify spacers; both changing the spacer and anchoring group with furan and cyanoacetic acid exhibited improved photoelectric and charge transfer performance compared to that of just changing the spacer or anchoring group. Besides, removing the spacer and introducing cyanoacetic acid generated the best charge transfer and JSC performance. ZL009 exhibited better charge transfer performance and should have the largest JSC among investigated single dyes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced molecular light capturing process and reduced electrons recombination distances by introducing graphene
ce:section.ce:para: The processes of electrons injection, recombination, dye regeneration and molecular light-capturing are key factors to evaluate molecular photoelectric performance. High ESP values in the ESP map from Fig. S12 and Fig. S13 mean that the place has a high tendency to react with redox couples to facilitate the dyes regeneration (Zhao et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2021). The high potential value in the Fukui map from Fig. S14 and Fig. S15 can precisely find nucleophilic sites. Combined with ESP and Fukui maps (Politzer et al., 2010), molecular nucleophilic places mainly focus on the donor parts and π-bridges. That is to say that molecular regeneration sites mainly focused on donor and π-bridges. The ESP surface area values of GQD/Dye composites are fairly even compared to a single dye from Fig. S16. Table 3 presents that the driving force for dye regeneration are in the following trend ZL009 (0.1960 eV) > ZL007 (0.1677 eV) > ZL008 (0.1633 eV) > ZL006 (0.1536 eV) > ZL003 (0.1533 eV). So, compared with ZL003, all molecular dye regeneration ability improved (Xie et al., 2016), especially for ZL009. When GQDs were introduced, the driving force for all composites' dye regeneration has been improved than single dyes. It can be drawn that the GQDs facilitates the regeneration ability of dyes. The process of electron injection is fast, and the electron injection time of molecules obtained through the Newns-Anderson model is presented in Fig. S17 (Preat, 2010). The time is in the range of 2.56–5.17 fs, therefore, electron injection is a speedy process. All molecular driving forces obtained are above 0.2 eV (Islam et al., 2003). Therefore, the electrons of all investigated dyes can be effectively injected into the CB of TiO2 during excitation. A lower electron recombination process is beneficial for generating enhanced JSC. Fig. S18 and Fig. S19 exhibit the molecular recombination distance. All investigated dyes have a long electron recombination distance between the hole and the surface of TiO2 (Clifford et al., 2004). ZL007 has the largest distance, which is beneficial for the improvement of molecular photoelectric performance. Upon incorporating GQDs, the GQD/Dye composites have reduced electron's recombination distances from Fig. S20 and Table S11. The LHE of all GQD/Dye composites has been improved compared to single dyes from Table 3, which can improve the composites' light capturing ability and induce higher JSC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Improved molecular photocurrent by the introduction of graphene
ce:section.ce:para:

where e is defined as unit charge; IPCE(λ) represents as monochromatic incident PCE; Is(λ) is the photon flux (Zhao et al., 2020; Ren et al., 2019; Grätzel, 2005; Ma et al., 2014); LHE(λ) is the LHE at a particular wavelength; Φinj and ηcoll present electron injection and collection efficiency (Lu et al., 2017; Mao et al., 2020; Ren et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2014); Γ is molecular adsorption value on the surface of semiconductor (mol∙cm−2), and according to the experimental reference, all molecular adsorption values basing on ZL003 of 5.2 × 10−8 mol/cm2 were used as a reference; δ(λ) stands for molecular absorption cross-section (cm2∙mol−1) (Zhao et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2014); ε(λ) is molar absorption coefficient (Zhao et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2014); Based on the marcus theory, all investigated molecules have relative longer recombination time, and the obtained all molecular ηcoll is tending to 1; △G0 is the driving force for the reaction of electrons injection obtained by the gap between the simulated conduction band of titanium dioxide (ECBM) and molecular LUMO value (Ren et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2014; Metri et al., 2012; Moser and Grätzel, 1993; Hutchison et al., 2005); λtotal; λ dye, λsolvent, λhole, and λele present total, solvent, inner, holes and electrons reorganization energy respectively (Ren et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2014; Lemaur et al., 2005; Marcus, 1965; Liu et al., 2018); rinj is distance between the cyanoacrylic (methanic) acid and the surface of TiO2; τinj is the required time for electron transfer towards the TiO2 surface basing on marcus theory (Ren et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2014), and τrelax is the relaxation lifetime (10 ps) as reported by Ren et al. (2019); Ma et al. (2014); Cherepy and Smestad (1997); τtrans stands for electron transfering time from the TiO2 film to the electrode usually (5 ps) (Ma et al., 2014), and τrec is the attenuation time; KET is the electron-transfer rate (Metri et al., 2012; Moser and Grätzel, 1993; Hutchison et al., 2005), which can be obtained by using the Marcus theory; β is the attenuation parameter (0.7) (Ren et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2014); A is a fixed value (Ma et al., 2014).

Table 4 and Table S12 present the photoelectric data. Fig. 6 vividly shows the obtained molecular photocurrent maps of single dyes and GQD/Dye composites. The JSC of single molecules are in the following order: ZL009 (23.13 mA cm−2) > ZL008 (22.88 mA cm−2) > ZL007 (21.85 mA cm−2) > ZL006 (19.65 mA cm−2) > ZL003 (19.03 mA cm−2); the GQD/Dye composites have the same trend but improved JSC than single dyes from Fig. 7 (a) and (b). To find a direct reason for the improved JSC, we make a comparison of molecular sunlight using the ability of single dyes and GQD/Dye composites. Fig. 8 (a) vividly shows that GQD/ZL003 has better sunlight utilizing ability and higher LHE among the visible region, contributing to improved JSC of GQD/ZL003. Fig. 8 (b-e) vividly exhibits GQD/ZL006-GQD/ZL009 composites' improved light capturing ability than single dyes. So it can be concluded that when introducing the GQDs, improvement of molecular sunlight utilizing ability results in the increase of JSC. Especially for GQD/ZL009, it shows the highest JSC among all the investigated molecules.

where ECB, q, KBT, nc, NCB, Eredox stand for conduction band of the semiconductor substrate (−4.0 eV), unit charge, thermal energy (0.025852 eV), electron numbers at CB of TiO2 (Ma et al., 2014; Marinado et al., 2009), active density states in the conduction band of TiO2 (7 × 1020 cm−3) (Ren et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2014; Marinado et al., 2009) and redox-oxidation couple (−4.8 eV). ΔΕCB is the movement of ECB (Ronca et al., 2013; Yusuf et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2020), and the calculated ΔΕCB is presented in Fig. S21. Table 5 shows the obtained photovoltaic data. All the photovoltage is in a range of 0.825–0.878 V. When introducing GQDs, the photovoltage of GQD/dye composites and single dyes have little difference, which can be attributed to the same conjugation type between TiO2 and anchoring groups.

The global I–V model curves of the DSSCs could be derived by using the following formulas (Ren et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2014; Grätzel, 2009; Jeon et al., 2016):

where IS is the reverse saturation current (Ren et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2014); V is photovoltage; I is current, and ImVm is the maximum power (Ren et al., 2019; Grätzel, 2005; Ma et al., 2014); FF is the fill factor (Ren et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2014; Jeon et al., 2016). The global I–V model curves from Eqs (12) and (13) of all investigated single dyes and GQD/Dye composites were presented in Fig. 9 (a) and Table S7, which demonstrates that JSC, VOC, FF and PCE of ZL003 are closed to the experiment data. When introducing GQDs, the GQD/Dye composites showed an improvement in PCE from Fig. 7 (c) and (d). By comparing the I-V curves and data in Table 6 and Fig. 9 (b-f), one can found the following trend: GQD/ZL008 (17.4%) > GQD/ZL009 (17.1%) > GQD/ZL007 (16.7%) > GQD/ZL006 (15.2%) = GQD/ZL003 (15.2%); ZL008 (16.7%) > ZL009 (16.6%) > ZL007 (16.4%) > ZL006 (14.6%) > ZL003 (14.5%). It can be concluded that the enhanced PCE of GQD/Dye composite was mainly associated with an improvement in JSC. Although ZL009 and GQD/ZL009 have the highest JSC value among single dyes and GQD/Dye composites, the PCE of GQD/ZL009 was not the largest due to the relatively small photovoltage. For ZL008 and GQD/ZL008, they have the relatively large JSC and VOC among all investigated single dyes and GQD/Dye composite, which leads to the ideal PCE of 16.7% (ZL008) and 17.4% (GQD/ZL008) higher than ZL003 (14.5%) under the same calculating model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Improved conjugation stability between the interface of dye and Tio2 for the introduction of graphene through FT-IR properties analysis
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR performances can reflect molecular stability (Gao et al., 2019). There are no negative frequencies of investigated single dyes, GQD/Dyes, and Dye/TiO2 composites. Therefore, all the investigated materials exhibit a stable structure. The FT-IR spectrums of GQDs, Dyes, TiO2, GQD/Dye, and Dye/TiO2 composites are shown in Fig. 10 , Fig. S22, and Fig. S23. The simulated morphology of (TiO2)9 cluster, the common connection type between dye and TiO2 conduction band, and the simulated structures of GQD/ZL008 and GQD/ZL008/TiO2 are vividly depicted in Fig. S24, Fig. S25 and Fig. S26, respectively. It can be found that as for GQD/ZL003, GQD/ZL006, GQD/ZL007, GQD/ZL008, and GQD/ZL009, new vibration peaks appear as to the frequencies and activities of (1729.67, 424.7978), (1729.59, 425.9630), (1729.11, 402.6220), (1727.97, 424.9101), and (1729.42, 425.2334) compared with single dyes and GQDs, which means that the dye and GQDs are connected well with each other within the amage group (Gao et al., 2019). The FT-IR spectrums of TiO2, dye and Dye/TiO2 composites are clearly illustrated in Fig. 10 (b) and Fig. S23. There is a sharp vibration peak of (COOH) group in the anchor groups of ZL003, ZL006, ZL007, ZL008, and ZL009 at the frequencies of 1783.05, 1787.35, 1782.08, 1777.66, 1777.13 cm−1, respectively. When the dye connected with TiO2. There exists an influential vibration group of (COOTi) (Burke et al., 2008) in the conjugation place of dye and TiO2, corresponding to the frequencies of 1454.78, 1468.25, 1456.34, 1413.18, and 1414.24 cm−1, respectively. It means that the (TiO2)9 cluster and dye have been connected by (COOTi) group through the bidentate cheating model, as presented in Fig. S25(b). The bidentate cheating model can provide a channel for electrons transfer from the dye to the (TiO2)9 cluster. Such phenomenon can contribute to better molecular photoelectric performance. The bond length between dyes (GQD/Dye) and TiO2 composite is in the range of (2.05802–2.09574 Å) from Table S13, showing that dyes and TiO2 are well connected. The interaction energy between the dye and TiO2 composite is in the range of (−23.8196 to −26.9565 kcal/mol). The interaction energy between GQD/dye and TiO2 composite is in the field of (−52.9702 to −56.0914 kcal/mol), which demonstrates that GQD/Dyes have more conjugation stability with TiO2 than single dyes. Therefore, GQDs enhanced the stability of molecular conjugation with TiO2, which benefits electron transfer from GQD/Dye to the CB of TiO2.

In this work, graphene was introduced to a series D-π-A type triazatruxene (TAT) organic compounds. A comprehensive evaluation model has been implemented to explore the GQD/Dye composites photoelectric performance, including Jsc, Voc, and PCE. It is found that owing to the introduction of GQDs, GQD/Dye composites exhibited improved molecular molar extinction coefficients, apparent high absorption peak in the ultraviolet region, higher molecular LHE, improved molecular regeneration ability and molecular charge transfer performance than single dyes. Besides, through the strategy of modifying the spacer and anchoring group of the original molecule ZL003, molecular LUMO has been tuned. As for single dyes, the approach of bringing in furan and cyanoacetic acid simultaneously (ZL008) showed the highest performance (16.7%) higher than that of ZL003 (14.5%). For ZL003, the PCE of 14.5% obtained through the built I-V evaluating model is in accordance with the experimental PCE of 13.4%. More importantly, GQD/ZL008 composite exhibits the most top PCE of 17.4% among all investigated dyes, which can be attributed to GQD/ZL008 and TiO2 having been firmly connected through the bidentate cheating model. The bidentate cheating model induced better electrons charge transfer performance from GQD/ZL008 to TiO2 conduction band. Besides, GQD/ZL008 presents a better light capturing ability to generate larger photocurrent and a relatively large photovoltage that leads to the excellent PCE. GQD/ZL008 can be considered as a promising candidate applied in DSSCs devices. This work provides a valuable theoretical basis for the performance evaluation and performance prediction of graphene doped solar power.
